en.extract:
The northern fantail (Rhipidura rufiventris) is a species of bird in the family Rhipiduridae. It is found in New Guinea, Timor, and northern Australia (from Broome in Western Australia to the Shire of Burdekin in Queensland). Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests and subtropical or tropical mangrove forests.


Taxonomy
The northern fantail was formally described in 1818 as Platyrhynchos rufiventris by the French ornithologist Louis Vieillot. The specific epithet is Modern Latin meaning "red-bellied" from Latin rufus meaning "ruddy" or "rufous" with venter, ventris meaning "belly". Vieillot mistakenly believed that the specimen had been collected in "Nouvelle-Hollande" (Australia). The type locality has been designated as the island of Timor. The specimen described by Vieillot had probably been collected in 1801 by René Maugé de Cely during the Baudin expedition to Australia. The northern fantail is now placed in the genus Rhipidura that was introduced in 1827 by Nicholas Vigors and Thomas Horsfield.

Twenty subspecies are recognised:

R. r. tenkatei Büttikofer, J, 1892 – Rote Island (eastern Lesser Sunda Islands)
R. r. rufiventris (Vieillot, LJP, 1818) – eastern Lesser Sunda Islands (Semau, Timor, and Jaco Island)
R. r. pallidiceps Hartert, EJO, 1904 – Wetar (eastern Lesser Sunda Islands)
R. r. hoedti Büttikofer, J, 1892 – eastern Lesser Sunda Islands (Sermata, Moa, Leti, Romang, and Damar Island)
R. r. assimilis Gray, GR, 1858 – Tayandu and Kai islands, southern Moluccas
R. r. finitima Hartert, EJO, 1918 – Watubela Islands, southern Moluccas
R. r. bouruensis Wallace, AR, 1863 – Buru Island (southern Moluccas)
R. r. cinerea Wallace, AR, 1865 – southern Moluccas (Seram, Ambon, and Boana)
R. r. obiensis Salvadori, AT, 1876 – Obi Island (northern Moluccas)
R. r. vidua Salvadori, AT & Turati, E, 1874 – Kofiau (Raja Ampat Islands, off western New Guinea)
R. r. gularis Müller, S, 1843 – New Guinea, including Yapen (Cenderawasih Bay, off northwestern New Guinea) and northern Torres Strait Islands (Boigu Island)
R. r. nigromentalis Hartert, EJO, 1898 – Misima and Tagula Island (Louisiade Archipelago, off southeastern New Guinea)
R. r. finschii Salvadori, AT, 1882 – New Britain and Duke of York (southeastern Bismarck Archipelago)
R. r. setosa (Quoy, JRC & Gaimard, JP, 1832) – New Hanover, Dyaul Island, and New Ireland (northeastern Bismarck Archipelago)
R. r. gigantea Stresemann, EFT, 1933 – Bismarck Archipelago (Lihir Island and Tabar Groups)
R. r. tangensis Mayr, E, 1955 – Bismarck Archipelago (Boang and Tanga Islands)
R. r. niveiventris Rothschild, LW & Hartert, EJO, 1914 – Admiralty Islands
R. r. mussai Rothschild, LW & Hartert, EJO, 1924 – Mussau Island, in St. Matthias Group (north-central Bismarck Archipelago)
R. r. isura Gould, J, 1841 – northern Australia (Kimberley region, northern Western Australia to Cape York Peninsula and Wet Tropics region, northern Queensland)
R. r. kordensis Meyer, AB, 1874 – Biak (Cenderawasih Bay, off northwestern New Guinea)
The subspecies R. r. kordensis has sometimes been considered as a separate species, the Biak fantail.

The '''northern fantail''' ('''''Rhipidura rufiventris''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Rhipiduridae]]. It is found in [[New Guinea]], [[Timor]], and northern [[Australia]] (from [[Broome, Western Australia|Broome]] in Western Australia to the [[Shire of Burdekin]] in [[Queensland]]).<ref>{{Cite web |title=Species profile—Rhipidura rufiventris (northern fantail) |url=https://apps.des.qld.gov.au/species-search/details/?id=1579 |access-date=2024-04-04 |website=Species information |publisher=Queensland Government}}</ref> Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s and subtropical or tropical mangrove forests.

==Taxonomy==
The northern fantail was [[species description|formally described]] in 1818 as ''Platyrhynchos rufiventris'' by the French ornithologist [[Louis Pierre Vieillot|Louis Vieillot]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Vieillot | first=Louis Pierre | author-link=Louis Pierre Vieillot | year=1818 | title=Nouveau dictionnaire d'histoire naturelle, appliquée aux arts, à l'agriculture, à l'économie rurale et domestique, à la médecine, etc. | edition=Nouvelle édition | volume=27 | location=Paris | publisher=Deterville | page=21 | language=French | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/19425806 }}</ref> The specific epithet is [[Modern Latin]] meaning "red-bellied" from [[Latin]] ''rufus'' meaning "ruddy" or "rufous" with ''venter'', ''ventris'' meaning "belly".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=rufiventris | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=rufiventris | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=6 January 2026 }}</ref> Vieillot mistakenly believed that the specimen had been collected in "Nouvelle-Hollande" (Australia). The [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] has been designated as the island of [[Timor]].<ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1986 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=11 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=538 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14484239 }}</ref> The specimen described by Vieillot had probably been collected in 1801 by [[René Maugé de Cely]] during the [[Baudin expedition to Australia]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Jansen | first=Justin J.F.J. | date=2017 | title=René Maugé’s ornithological collections from Kupang Bay, West-Timor, Indonesia, August-November 1801, with special regard to type-specimens | journal=Zoosystematics and Evolution | volume=93 | issue=2 | pages=467–492 | doi=10.3897/zse.93.19964| doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{ cite journal | last=Jansen | first=Justin J.F.J. | date=2017 | title=Towards the resolution of long-standing issues regarding birds collected during the Baudin expedition to Australia and Timor (1800–1804): specimens still present, and their importance to Australian ornithology | journal=Journal of the National Museum (Prague), Natural History Series | volume=186 | pages=51-84 | doi=10.2478/jzh-2018-0003 | doi-access=free }}</ref> The northern fantail is now placed in the genus ''[[Rhipidura]]'' that was introduced in 1827 by [[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Nicholas Vigors]] and [[Thomas Horsfield]].<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=7 January 2026 }}</ref>

[[File:Northern Fantail.1.jpg|thumb|Fogg Dam, Middle Point, Northern Territory, Australia]]
Twenty [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist/>
* ''R. r. tenkatei'' [[Johann Büttikofer|Büttikofer, J]], 1892 – [[Rote Island]] (eastern [[Lesser Sunda Islands]])
* ''R. r. rufiventris'' ([[Louis Pierre Vieillot|Vieillot, LJP]], 1818) – eastern [[Lesser Sunda Islands]] ([[Semau]], [[Timor]], and [[Jaco Island]])
* ''R. r. pallidiceps'' [[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, EJO]], 1904 – [[Wetar]] (eastern [[Lesser Sunda Islands]])
* ''R. r. hoedti'' Büttikofer, J, 1892 – eastern [[Lesser Sunda Islands]] (Sermata, [[Moa (island)|Moa]], [[Leti (island)|Leti]], [[Romang (island)|Romang]], and [[Damar Island]])
* ''R. r. assimilis'' [[George Robert Gray|Gray, GR]], 1858 – Tayandu and Kai islands, southern [[Maluku Islands|Moluccas]]
* ''R. r. finitima'' Hartert, EJO, 1918 – Watubela Islands, southern [[Maluku Islands|Moluccas]]
* ''R. r. bouruensis'' [[Alfred Russel Wallace|Wallace, AR]], 1863 – Buru Island (southern [[Maluku Islands|Moluccas]])
* ''R. r. cinerea'' Wallace, AR, 1865 – southern [[Maluku Islands|Moluccas]] ([[Seram Island|Seram]], [[Ambon Island|Ambon]], and [[Boana]])
* ''R. r. obiensis'' [[Tommaso Salvadori|Salvadori, AT]], 1876 – Obi Island (northern [[Maluku Islands|Moluccas]])
* ''R. r. vidua'' Salvadori, AT & Turati, E, 1874 – [[Kofiau]] ([[Raja Ampat Islands]], off western [[New Guinea]])
* ''R. r. gularis'' [[Salomon Müller|Müller, S]], 1843 – [[New Guinea]], including [[Yapen]] ([[Cenderawasih Bay]], off northwestern [[New Guinea]]) and northern Torres Strait Islands (Boigu Island)
* ''R. r. nigromentalis'' Hartert, EJO, 1898 – [[Misima]] and [[Tagula Island]] ([[Louisiade Archipelago]], off southeastern [[New Guinea]])
* ''R. r. finschii'' Salvadori, AT, 1882 – [[New Britain]] and Duke of York (southeastern [[Bismarck Archipelago]])
* ''R. r. setosa'' ([[Jean René Constant Quoy|Quoy, JRC]] & [[Joseph Paul Gaimard|Gaimard, JP]], 1832) – New Hanover, [[Dyaul Island]], and [[New Ireland (island)|New Ireland]] (northeastern [[Bismarck Archipelago]])
* ''R. r. gigantea'' [[Erwin Stresemann|Stresemann, EFT]], 1933 – [[Bismarck Archipelago]] ([[Lihir Island]] and Tabar Groups)
* ''R. r. tangensis'' [[Ernst Mayr|Mayr, E]], 1955 – [[Bismarck Archipelago]] (Boang and [[Tanga Islands]])
* ''R. r. niveiventris'' [[Walter Rothschild, 2nd Baron Rothschild|Rothschild, LW]] & Hartert, EJO, 1914 – [[Admiralty Islands]]
* ''R. r. mussai'' Rothschild, LW & Hartert, EJO, 1924 – [[Mussau Island]], in St. Matthias Group (north-central [[Bismarck Archipelago]])
* ''R. r. isura'' [[John Gould|Gould, J]], 1841 – northern Australia ([[Kimberley (Western Australia)|Kimberley]] region, northern [[Western Australia]] to [[Cape York Peninsula]] and [[Wet Tropics of Queensland|Wet Tropics]] region, northern [[Queensland]])
* ''R. r. kordensis'' [[Adolf Bernhard Meyer|Meyer, AB]], 1874 – [[Biak]] ([[Cenderawasih Bay]], off northwestern [[New Guinea]])

The subspecies ''R. r. kordensis'' has sometimes been considered as a separate species, the Biak fantail.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Orioles, drongos, fantails | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/orioles/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=7 January 2025}}</ref>
